concerns.text: FR-005 states: 'System MUST train a Ridge Regression model using [deferred] of subjects for training'. The placeholder '[deferred]' is syntactically incomplete and semantically ambiguous in a Functional Requirement. It does not specify the *metric* being deferred (e.g., 'percentage', 'count', 'ratio') nor the *reference* (e.g., 'total subjects', 'training split'). This contradicts the spec's own guidance in SC-001/SC-002 which states planning docs must name the metric and reference even if the value is deferred. A requirement cannot be 'MUST' if the subject of the action is undefined.

FR-006 states: 'System MUST evaluate model performance ... on a [deferred] held-out test set'. Similar to FR-005, the phrase '[deferred] held-out test set' is grammatically broken and logically incomplete. It fails to define what aspect is deferred (size? subject count? percentage?). This creates a contradiction with the 'Independent Test' in User Story 3 which implies a specific 'held-out test set' exists, yet the FR defining that set is incomplete.

Acceptance Scenario 1 in User Story 3 reads: '...evaluated on a [deferred] held-out test set.' This repeats the incomplete placeholder found in FR-005/FR-006. While placeholders are allowed for empirical values, the syntax '[deferred] held-out test set' is not a valid equivalent form of a deferred metric. It should be phrased as 'evaluated on a held-out test set (size deferred)' or similar to maintain grammatical integrity and clarity.

Acceptance Scenario 3 states: '...automatically switches to a chunked loading strategy or subsampling method...'. This introduces a scope shift. The User Story 1 description and FR-001/FR-002 focus on downloading and preprocessing the *specific* OpenNeuro ds000246 dataset (approx 7GB). The acceptance scenario generalizes to 'a dataset larger than available RAM', implying a generic pipeline capability. While not a direct contradiction, it creates ambiguity: is the system required to handle *any* large dataset, or just this specific one? The 'Independent Test' only mentions testing on the target runner with the specific dataset, suggesting the generalization is out of scope.
